title: The space between lines
checkLabels.t8: The heading scales with its font
checkLabels.t11: The heading is still dark red
why.empty: The editor is empty. Hit the bin and start over.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The heading lines still overlap.
hint2.untouched: One number changes, one is added.
why.unlinked: The page cannot find styles.css. You changed the link line.
hint2.unlinked: Hit the bin and start over.
why.used-inline: You used style in the tag. That is lesson 01 again.
why.touched-html: You edited the HTML. Here you work only in styles.css.
why.no-note: There is no element with the note class.
why.no-page: There is no element with the page class.
why.fixed-line: There is still a line height set in pixels.
hint2.fixed-line: That number is inherited as a number of pixels, not as a ratio. That is where the heading problem comes from.
why.body-line: The body text has no sensible line spacing.
hint2.body-line: One and a half times the font size is the usual figure for reading on screen.
hint2.not-scaling: Its font is 32 pixels and the room for a line is 24. The letters have nowhere to fit.
hint3.not-scaling: The heading sets nothing of its own — it inherits. The question is WHAT it inherits: a finished pixel value, or a ratio it works out itself.
hint4.not-scaling: A number with no unit is inherited as a ratio and every element multiplies it by ITS OWN size.
why.note-loose: The note has far too much space between its lines.
hint2.note-loose: It uses a larger font and is short. One and a half is too much for it.
why.h1-broken: The heading is no longer dark red.
blocks.0.text: Open the preview. The heading runs to two lines and the letters nearly touch — the top line sits on the bottom one. Its rule says nothing about spacing. The culprit is in body, four lines above.
blocks.2.text: Line height can be set two ways, and the difference is only in how it is inherited:
blocks.4.text: With a unit, descendants inherit the finished value: 24 pixels, however large their own font is. The heading uses a 32 pixel font and gets a 24 pixel line — hence the overlap. With no unit, descendants inherit the RATIO and each multiplies it by its own size: the heading works out 32 times 1.5 and gets 48.
blocks.5.text: With a unit you inherit the result. Without one you inherit the rule.
blocks.7.text: There is no single right number, but there are limits beyond which reading suffers:
blocks.8.items.0: Body text: between 1.4 and 1.6. The browser default is around 1.2 and is cramped for long text.
blocks.8.items.1: Headings: between 1.1 and 1.3. A large font needs less air, or the lines fall apart into separate strips.
blocks.8.items.2: Never below 1.4 for paragraphs somebody will actually read.
blocks.9.text: Why this is accessibility, not taste
blocks.10.text: One of the WCAG criteria says a page must stay readable when a reader raises the line spacing to at least 1.5 times the font size themselves. People with dyslexia and failing eyesight really do this — there are extensions and user stylesheets for exactly that. If you have nailed the spacing down in pixels, their setting fights yours and usually loses. A unitless number does not resist.
blocks.12.text: Give the page line spacing that grows with the font instead of being nailed down. The body text should breathe, and the note in the larger font should be tighter — 1.2.
